$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \mathrm{t} / \min & {[\mathrm{A}] / \mathrm{M}} \\ \hline 0 & 0.6500 \\ \hline x & 0.0650 \\ \hline 20 & 0.00065 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$x=$ $\_\_\_\_$ min. (Nearest integer)
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction, the integrated rate law is

$$ k=\frac{2.303}{t}\log\frac{[A]_0}{[A]_t} $$

We use the data at $t=20$ min:

  • Initial concentration, $[A]_0=0.6500\ \text{M}$

  • Concentration at $20$ min, $[A]_{20}=0.00065\ \text{M}$

So,

$$ k=\frac{2.303}{20}\log\frac{0.6500}{0.00065} $$

Now,

$$ \frac{0.6500}{0.00065}=1000 $$

and

$$ \log 1000 = 3 $$

Therefore,

$$ k=\frac{2.303}{20}\times 3 $$

$$ k=\frac{6.909}{20} $$

$$ k=0.34545\ \text{min}^{-1} $$

Now we use the concentration at time $x$:

  • $[A]_x=0.0650\ \text{M}$

Again,

$$ k=\frac{2.303}{x}\log\frac{[A]_0}{[A]_x} $$

$$ 0.34545=\frac{2.303}{x}\log\frac{0.6500}{0.0650} $$

Now,

$$ \frac{0.6500}{0.0650}=10 $$

and

$$ \log 10 = 1 $$

So,

$$ 0.34545=\frac{2.303}{x} $$

Hence,

$$ x=\frac{2.303}{0.34545} $$

$$ x\approx 6.67\ \text{min} $$

Nearest integer:

$$ \boxed{7\ \text{min}} $$

questions.questionText:
An electrochemical cell, consist of the following two redox couples, $\mathrm{M}^{x+} (\mathrm{aq}) / \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{s})\left[\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\Theta}=+0.15 \mathrm{~V}\right]$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq}) / \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})\left[\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\Theta}=-0.036 \mathrm{~V}\right]$. The cell EMF $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}\right)$ is recorded to be 0.2057 V . If the reaction quotient of the electrochemical reaction is found to be $10^{-2}$, then the value of $x$ is

$\_\_\_\_$ .(Nearest integer)

[Given : M is a p-block metal and $\frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.059 \mathrm{~V}$ ]
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
For the cell, the two reduction potentials are:

$$ \mathrm{M}^{x+} + x e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{M}(s), \qquad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = +0.15\,\text{V} $$

and

$$ \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + 3 e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}(s), \qquad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = -0.036\,\text{V} $$

Since the reduction potential of $\mathrm{M}^{x+}/\mathrm{M}$ is greater, it will act as the cathode.

So,

  • Cathode: $\mathrm{M}^{x+} + x e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{M}(s)$

  • Anode: $\mathrm{Fe}(s) \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + 3e^-$

Hence,

$$ E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = 0.15 - (-0.036) = 0.186\,\text{V} $$

Now use the Nernst equation:

$$ E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.059}{n}\log Q $$

Given:

$$ E_{\text{cell}} = 0.2057\,\text{V}, \qquad Q = 10^{-2} $$

Since $\log 10^{-2} = -2$,

$$ 0.2057 = 0.186 - \frac{0.059}{n}(-2) $$

$$ 0.2057 = 0.186 + \frac{0.118}{n} $$

$$ 0.2057 - 0.186 = \frac{0.118}{n} $$

$$ 0.0197 = \frac{0.118}{n} $$

$$ n = \frac{0.118}{0.0197} \approx 5.99 \approx 6 $$

So the number of electrons transferred in the balanced cell reaction is

$$ n = 6 $$

Now, for balancing electrons:

  • Oxidation of iron gives $3$ electrons

  • Reduction of $\mathrm{M}^{x+}$ consumes $x$ electrons

Therefore, total electrons transferred in the balanced equation will be $\mathrm{LCM}(3,x)$.

Given this is $6$, we get:

$$ \mathrm{LCM}(3,x)=6 $$

Possible values of $x$ are $2$ or $6$.

Now it is given that $\mathrm{M}$ is a p-block metal. Common p-block metals generally form ions like $2^+$, $3^+$, $4^+$, etc. Among the possible values, the most suitable is

$$ x=2 $$

So, the nearest integer value of $x$ is

$$ \boxed{2} $$

questions.questionText: In sulphur estimation, $2.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$ of an organic compound $(\mathrm{X})$ (molar mass $76 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate (molar mass $233 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ). The percentage of sulphur in the compound $(\mathrm{X})$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-1} \%$ (Nearest integer)
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
We are given the number of moles and the molar mass of compound (X). We can find its total mass using the formula:

$$ \text{Mass} = \text{Moles} \times \text{Molar Mass} $$

$$ \text{Mass of compound (X)} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol} \times 76 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \text{Mass of compound (X)} = 152 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g} = 0.152 \text{ g} $$

In the Carius method, the sulphur in the organic compound is quantitatively converted into barium sulphate ($ \mathrm{BaSO_4} $).

According to the molar masses:

$1 \text{ mole of } \mathrm{BaSO_4} (233 \text{ g}) \text{ contains } 1 \text{ mole of Sulphur } (32 \text{ g})$.

Therefore, the mass of sulphur in $ 0.4813 \text{ g} $ of $ \mathrm{BaSO_4} $ is:

$$ \text{Mass of Sulphur} = \frac{32}{233} \times \text{Mass of } \mathrm{BaSO_4} $$

$$ \text{Mass of Sulphur} = \frac{32}{233} \times 0.4813 \text{ g} $$

$$ \% \text{ of Sulphur} = \frac{\text{Mass of Sulphur}}{\text{Total mass of compound (X)}} \times 100 $$

Substitute the values we found in Steps 1 and 2:

$$ \% \text{ of Sulphur} = \left( \frac{\frac{32}{233} \times 0.4813}{0.152} \right) \times 100 $$

$$ \% \text{ of Sulphur} = \frac{32 \times 0.4813 \times 100}{233 \times 0.152} $$

$$ \% \text{ of Sulphur} = \frac{1540.16}{35.416} $$

$$ \% \text{ of Sulphur} \approx 43.4877 \% $$

We need to express the answer in the form of $ \_\_\_\_ \times 10^{-1} \% $. Let us rewrite our percentage:

$$ 43.4877 \% = 434.877 \times 10^{-1} \% $$

When we round $ 434.877 $ to the nearest integer, we get $ 435 $.

The percentage of sulphur in the compound (X) is 435 $ \times 10^{-1} \% $.

questions.questionText:
For the following reaction at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and at 2 atm pressure,

$$ 2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) $$

$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$ is $50 \%$ dissociated.

The magnitude of standard free energy change at this temperature is $x$.

$x=$ $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ [Nearest integer].

Given : $\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}, \log 2=0.30, \log 3=0.48, \ln 10=2.303$, ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}+273=\mathrm{K}$
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
For the reaction

$$ 2\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4(g) + \mathrm{O}_2(g) $$

we have to find the magnitude of standard free energy change, that is, $|\Delta G^\circ|$.

We use the relation

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p $$

So first, let us calculate $K_p$.

1. Take 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5$ initially

Given that $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5$ is $50\%$ dissociated.

So, initial moles of $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5 = 1$

Dissociated moles $= 0.5$

Now from the reaction,

$$ 2\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 2\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4 + \mathrm{O}_2 $$

If $2$ moles of $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5$ dissociate, then:

  • $2$ moles of $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4$ are formed

  • $1$ mole of $\mathrm{O}_2$ is formed

Therefore, if $0.5$ mole of $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5$ dissociates:

  • $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4$ formed $= 0.5$ mole

  • $\mathrm{O}_2$ formed $= 0.25$ mole

So at equilibrium:

$$ n(\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5)=1-0.5=0.5 $$

$$ n(\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4)=0.5 $$

$$ n(\mathrm{O}_2)=0.25 $$

Total moles at equilibrium:

$$ n_\text{total}=0.5+0.5+0.25=1.25 $$

2. Calculate mole fractions

$$ y_{\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5}=\frac{0.5}{1.25}=0.4 $$

$$ y_{\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4}=\frac{0.5}{1.25}=0.4 $$

$$ y_{\mathrm{O}_2}=\frac{0.25}{1.25}=0.2 $$

Given total pressure $P=2$ atm.

Hence partial pressures are:

$$ P_{\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5}=0.4 \times 2 = 0.8 \text{ atm} $$

$$ P_{\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4}=0.4 \times 2 = 0.8 \text{ atm} $$

$$ P_{\mathrm{O}_2}=0.2 \times 2 = 0.4 \text{ atm} $$

3. Expression for $K_p$

For the reaction

$$ 2\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4(g)+\mathrm{O}_2(g) $$

$$ K_p=\frac{(P_{\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4})^2(P_{\mathrm{O}_2})}{(P_{\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_5})^2} $$

Substitute the values:

$$ K_p=\frac{(0.8)^2(0.4)}{(0.8)^2}=0.4 $$

So,

$$ K_p=0.4 $$

4. Calculate $\Delta G^\circ$

Temperature:

$$ T=50+273=323\text{ K} $$

Now,

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -8.314 \times 323 \times \ln(0.4) $$

Now,

$$ 0.4=\frac{2}{5} $$

So,

$$ \ln(0.4)=\ln 2 - \ln 5 $$

Also,

$$ \log 5 = 1-\log 2 = 1-0.30=0.70 $$

Hence,

$$ \log(0.4)=\log 4 -1 = 2\log 2 -1 = 0.60-1=-0.40 $$

Therefore,

$$ \ln(0.4)=2.303 \times (-0.40)=-0.9212 $$

Now,

$$ \Delta G^\circ = -8.314 \times 323 \times (-0.9212) $$

$$ \Delta G^\circ \approx 2472 \text{ J mol}^{-1} $$

So the magnitude is

$$ x=2472 \text{ J mol}^{-1} $$

Final Answer:

$$ \boxed{2472} $$

questions.questionText:
The shortest distance between the lines

$$ \vec{r}=\left(\frac{1}{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\frac{8}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) $$

and $\vec{r}=\left(-\frac{2}{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{1}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}), \lambda, \mu \in \mathbb{R}$, is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For two skew lines,

$$ \vec r=\vec a_1+\lambda \vec b_1 \quad \text{and} \quad \vec r=\vec a_2+\mu \vec b_2, $$

the shortest distance is

$$ d=\frac{|(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)\cdot (\vec b_1\times \vec b_2)|}{|\vec b_1\times \vec b_2|}. $$

Now compare with the given lines.

First line:

$$ \vec r=\left(\frac13 \hat i+2\hat j+\frac83 \hat k\right)+\lambda(2\hat i-5\hat j+6\hat k) $$

So,

$$ \vec a_1=\left(\frac13,2,\frac83\right), \qquad \vec b_1=(2,-5,6) $$

Second line:

$$ \vec r=\left(-\frac23 \hat i-\frac13 \hat k\right)+\mu(\hat j-\hat k) $$

So,

$$ \vec a_2=\left(-\frac23,0,-\frac13\right), \qquad \vec b_2=(0,1,-1) $$

Now find

$$ \vec a_2-\vec a_1 =\left(-\frac23-\frac13,\;0-2,\;-\frac13-\frac83\right) =(-1,-2,-3) $$

Next, calculate $\vec b_1\times \vec b_2$:

$$ \vec b_1\times \vec b_2 = \begin{vmatrix} \hat i & \hat j & \hat k\\ 2 & -5 & 6\\ 0 & 1 & -1 \end{vmatrix} $$

$$ = \hat i\big[(-5)(-1)-6(1)\big] -\hat j\big[2(-1)-6(0)\big] +\hat k\big[2(1)-(-5)(0)\big] $$

$$ = \hat i(5-6)-\hat j(-2)+\hat k(2) = -\hat i+2\hat j+2\hat k $$

So,

$$ \vec b_1\times \vec b_2=(-1,2,2) $$

Its magnitude is

$$ |\vec b_1\times \vec b_2| =\sqrt{(-1)^2+2^2+2^2} =\sqrt{1+4+4} =3 $$

Now find the scalar triple product:

$$ (\vec a_2-\vec a_1)\cdot (\vec b_1\times \vec b_2) =(-1,-2,-3)\cdot(-1,2,2) $$

$$ = (-1)(-1)+(-2)(2)+(-3)(2) =1-4-6 =-9 $$

Therefore,

$$ d=\frac{|-9|}{3}=3 $$

Hence, the shortest distance is

$$ \boxed{3} $$

So, the correct option is Option B.

questions.questionText: If $\left(2 \alpha+1, \alpha^2-3 \alpha, \frac{\alpha-1}{2}\right)$ is the image of $(\alpha, 2 \alpha, 1)$ in the line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z}{1}$, then the possible value(s) of $\alpha$ is (are)
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\therefore \mathrm{P}$ is mid point of AB and lies on the given line

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{3 \alpha+1}{2}, \frac{\alpha^2-\alpha}{2}, \frac{\alpha+1}{4}\right) $$

Now put coordinates of P in the line

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{3 \alpha+1}{2}-2}{3}=\frac{\frac{\alpha^2-\alpha}{2}-1}{2}=\frac{\alpha+1}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha-1}{2}=\frac{\alpha+1}{4}=\frac{\alpha^2-\alpha-2}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=3 \end{aligned} $$

Now for $\alpha=3$, clearly AB is perpendicular to $3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\mathrm{k}$

for $\alpha=3$ point B is image of point A

questions.questionText: $\max\limits_{0 \leq x \leq \pi}\left(16 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos ^3\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$E = 16\\sin\frac{x}{2}\cos^3\frac{x}{2}$$

$$E = 4\sin x\,[1 + \cos x]$$

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = 4[\cos x + \cos 2x]$$

$$= 8\cos\frac{3x}{2}\cos\frac{x}{2} = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \cos\frac{3x}{2} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \cos\frac{x}{2} = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow x = \left\{\frac{\pi}{3}, \pi\right\} \text{ are critical points of the function}$$

$$E(0) = 0$$

$$E(\pi) = 0$$

$$E\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = 3\sqrt{3}$$

$\therefore$ maximum value of $E = 3\sqrt{3}$

questions.questionText: The area of the region bounded by the curves $x+3 y^2=0$ and $x+4 y^2=1$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given curves are

$$ x+3y^2=0 \quad \text{and} \quad x+4y^2=1 $$

First, write them in the form $x=$:

$$ x=-3y^2 $$

and

$$ x=1-4y^2 $$

To find the bounded area, we first find their points of intersection.

Set the two values of $x$ equal:

$$ -3y^2=1-4y^2 $$

$$ y^2=1 $$

$$ y=\pm 1 $$

So the curves intersect at $y=-1$ and $y=1$.

Now, for a fixed value of $y$, the right curve is

$$ x=1-4y^2 $$

and the left curve is

$$ x=-3y^2 $$

So the horizontal width of the region is

$$ (1-4y^2)-(-3y^2)=1-y^2 $$

Hence, area of the enclosed region is

$$ A=\int_{-1}^{1}(1-y^2)\,dy $$

Now evaluate:

$$ A=\left[y-\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{1} $$

$$ A=\left(1-\frac{1}{3}\right)-\left(-1+\frac{1}{3}\right) $$

$$ A=\frac{2}{3}-\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right) $$

$$ A=\frac{4}{3} $$

Therefore, the required area is

$$ \boxed{\frac{4}{3}} $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option C}} $$

questions.questionText: In the expansion of $\left(9 x-\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{x}}\right)^{18}, x>0$, if the term independent of $x$ is (221)k, then k is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the expansion of

$$ \left(9x-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^{18}, $$

we use the general term in the binomial expansion:

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{18}{r}(9x)^{18-r}\left(-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^r $$

Now simplify the power of $x$ in this term.

Since

$$ (9x)^{18-r}=9^{18-r}x^{18-r} $$

and

$$ \left(\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^r=\frac{1}{3^r x^{r/2}}, $$

so the power of $x$ in the general term is

$$ x^{18-r-\frac r2}=x^{18-\frac{3r}{2}}. $$

For the term independent of $x$, the power of $x$ must be zero:

$$ 18-\frac{3r}{2}=0 $$

$$ \frac{3r}{2}=18 $$

$$ r=12 $$

So the required term is the $(12+1)$th term, i.e. $T_{13}$.

Now compute it:

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12}(9x)^6\left(-\frac{1}{3\sqrt{x}}\right)^{12} $$

Since the power of $x$ becomes zero, only the numerical coefficient remains:

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12}\cdot 9^6 \cdot \frac{1}{3^{12}} $$

Also,

$$ 9^6=(3^2)^6=3^{12} $$

So,

$$ T_{13}=\binom{18}{12} $$

and

$$ \binom{18}{12}=\binom{18}{6}=\frac{18!}{6!\,12!}=18564 $$

Given that the term independent of $x$ is $(221)k$, we have

$$ 221k=18564 $$

Thus,

$$ k=\frac{18564}{221}=84 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{84} $$

Hence, Option A is correct.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{S}=\left\{z \in \mathrm{C}: z^2+4 z+16=0\right\}$. Then $\sum\limits_{z \in \mathrm{~S}}|z+\sqrt{3} \mathrm{i}|^2$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Solve the quadratic equation first:

$$ z^2+4z+16=0 $$

Using the quadratic formula,

$$ z=\frac{-4\pm\sqrt{16-64}}{2} =\frac{-4\pm\sqrt{-48}}{2} =\frac{-4\pm 4\sqrt{3}i}{2} =-2\pm 2\sqrt{3}i $$

So the set is

$$ S=\{-2+2\sqrt{3}i,\,-2-2\sqrt{3}i\} $$

Now we need to find

$$ \sum_{z\in S}|z+\sqrt{3}i|^2 $$

That is,

$$ |-2+2\sqrt{3}i+\sqrt{3}i|^2 + |-2-2\sqrt{3}i+\sqrt{3}i|^2 $$

Simplify each term:

  1. For $z=-2+2\sqrt{3}i$,

$$ z+\sqrt{3}i=-2+3\sqrt{3}i $$

Hence,

$$ |-2+3\sqrt{3}i|^2=(-2)^2+(3\sqrt{3})^2=4+27=31 $$

  1. For $z=-2-2\sqrt{3}i$,

$$ z+\sqrt{3}i=-2-\sqrt{3}i $$

Hence,

$$ |-2-\sqrt{3}i|^2=(-2)^2+(-\sqrt{3})^2=4+3=7 $$

Therefore,

$$ \sum_{z\in S}|z+\sqrt{3}i|^2=31+7=38 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{38} $$

Option D

questions.questionText: The integral $\int\limits_0^1 \cot ^{-1}\left(1+x+x^2\right) d x$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let

$$ I=\int_0^1 \cot^{-1}(1+x+x^2)\,dx $$

We will use the standard identity

$$ \cot^{-1} t=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{t}\right)\quad \text{for } t>0 $$

Since $1+x+x^2>0$ on $[0,1]$, we can write

$$ I=\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1+x+x^2}\right)\,dx $$

Now use the formula

$$ \tan^{-1} a-\tan^{-1} b=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a-b}{1+ab}\right) $$

We try to express

$$ \frac{1}{1+x+x^2} $$

in a suitable form. Observe that

$$ \tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{(x+1)-x}{1+x(x+1)}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1+x+x^2}\right) $$

Hence,

$$ \cot^{-1}(1+x+x^2)=\tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x $$

So,

$$ I=\int_0^1 \left[\tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x\right]dx $$

Therefore,

$$ I=\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(x+1)\,dx-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx $$

In the first integral, put $u=x+1$. Then when $x=0$, $u=1$, and when $x=1$, $u=2$. So

$$ \int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(x+1)\,dx=\int_1^2 \tan^{-1}u\,du $$

Thus,

$$ I=\int_1^2 \tan^{-1}u\,du-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \int_1^2 \tan^{-1}x\,dx-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx $$

Now use

$$ \int \tan^{-1}x\,dx=x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2) $$

So,

$$ \int_1^2 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_1^2 $$

and

$$ \int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_0^1 $$

Hence,

$$ I= \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_1^2 - \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_0^1 $$

This becomes

$$ I= \left(2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5\right) - \left(\tan^{-1}1-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) -\left(\tan^{-1}1-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) $$

since at $x=0$, the value is $0$.

Now $\tan^{-1}1=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, so

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5-2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) $$

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5-\frac{\pi}{2}+\log 2 $$

Now combine the logarithms:

$$ \log 2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5 = \frac{1}{2}\log 4-\frac{1}{2}\log 5 = \frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{5}{4}\right) $$

Therefore,

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2} $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log_e\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

questions.questionText:
If $\alpha=1$ and $\beta=1+i \sqrt{2}$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$ are two roots of the equation

$x^3+a x^2+b x+c=0, a, b, \in \mathbb{R}$, then $\int_{-1}^1\left(x^3+a x^2+b x+c\right) d x$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since the coefficients $a,b,c$ are real, complex roots occur in conjugate pairs.

Given two roots are:

$$ \alpha=1,\qquad \beta=1+i\sqrt{2} $$

So the third root must be:

$$ 1-i\sqrt{2} $$

Hence the cubic polynomial is:

$$ x^3+ax^2+bx+c=(x-1)\bigl(x-(1+i\sqrt{2})\bigr)\bigl(x-(1-i\sqrt{2})\bigr) $$

Now first multiply the conjugate factors:

$$ \bigl(x-(1+i\sqrt{2})\bigr)\bigl(x-(1-i\sqrt{2})\bigr) $$

This is of the form:

$$ (A-iB)(A+iB)=A^2+B^2 $$

with

$$ A=x-1,\qquad B=\sqrt{2} $$

So,

$$ \bigl(x-(1+i\sqrt{2})\bigr)\bigl(x-(1-i\sqrt{2})\bigr) =(x-1)^2+2 $$

Therefore,

$$ x^3+ax^2+bx+c=(x-1)\bigl((x-1)^2+2\bigr) $$

Let $y=x-1$. Then:

$$ (x-1)\bigl((x-1)^2+2\bigr)=(x-1)^3+2(x-1) $$

Now expand:

$$ (x-1)^3=x^3-3x^2+3x-1 $$

So,

$$ x^3+ax^2+bx+c=x^3-3x^2+3x-1+2x-2 $$

$$ =x^3-3x^2+5x-3 $$

Now evaluate:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(x^3+ax^2+bx+c)\,dx = \int_{-1}^{1}(x^3-3x^2+5x-3)\,dx $$

Using symmetry:

  • $x^3$ is an odd function, so

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}x^3\,dx=0 $$

  • $5x$ is also odd, so

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}5x\,dx=0 $$

Thus,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(x^3-3x^2+5x-3)\,dx = \int_{-1}^{1}(-3x^2-3)\,dx $$

Now,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}x^2\,dx=\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{1}=\frac13-\left(-\frac13\right)=\frac23 $$

Therefore,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(-3x^2)\,dx=-3\cdot \frac23=-2 $$

Also,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}(-3)\,dx=-3(2)=-6 $$

So total value:

$$ -2-6=-8 $$

Hence,

$$ \int_{-1}^{1}\left(x^3+ax^2+bx+c\right)\,dx=-8 $$

The correct option is:

$$ \boxed{-8} $$

So, Option C is correct.

questions.questionText:
Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation :

$$ \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{6 x^2+\left(3 x^2+2 x^3+4\right) e^{-2 x}}{\left(x^3+2\right)\left(2+e^{-2 x}\right)}\right) y=2+e^{-2 x} $$

$x \in(-1,2)$, satisfying $y(0)=\frac{3}{2}$. If $y(1)=\alpha\left(2+e^{-2}\right)$, then $\alpha$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given differential equation is

$$ \frac{dy}{dx}+\left(\frac{6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x}}{(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})}\right)y=2+e^{-2x} $$

with initial condition

$$ y(0)=\frac{3}{2}. $$

We have to find $y(1)=\alpha(2+e^{-2})$.

Step 1: Compare with linear differential equation

This is of the form

$$ \frac{dy}{dx}+P(x)y=Q(x), $$

where

$$ P(x)=\frac{6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x}}{(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})}, \qquad Q(x)=2+e^{-2x}. $$

To solve, we find the integrating factor:

$$ \text{I.F.}=e^{\int P(x)\,dx}. $$

Step 2: Simplify $P(x)$

Notice that

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(x^3+2)=3x^2, $$

and

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(2+e^{-2x})=-2e^{-2x}. $$

Now consider

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})\right]. $$

Using product rule,

$$ = (3x^2)(2+e^{-2x})+(x^3+2)(-2e^{-2x}) $$

$$ = 6x^2+3x^2e^{-2x}-2x^3e^{-2x}-4e^{-2x}. $$

This does not match directly. So let us try another observation.

Write numerator as

$$ 6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x}. $$

Now check

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})\right] =6x^2+\left(3x^2-2x^3-4\right)e^{-2x}. $$

Hence if we take

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})^{-1}\right] $$

it becomes complicated. So instead, let us look for a useful factorization.

Observe that

$$ 6x^2+\left(3x^2+2x^3+4\right)e^{-2x} =3x^2(2+e^{-2x})+(x^3+2)\cdot 2e^{-2x}. $$

Therefore

$$ P(x)=\frac{3x^2(2+e^{-2x})+(x^3+2)\cdot 2e^{-2x}}{(x^3+2)(2+e^{-2x})} $$

$$ =\frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}+\frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}. $$

So,

$$ P(x)=\frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}+\frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}. $$

Step 3: Find integrating factor

Now

$$ \int P(x)\,dx=\int \frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}\,dx+\int \frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}\,dx. $$

For the first integral:

Let $u=x^3+2$, then $du=3x^2dx$.

So,

$$ \int \frac{3x^2}{x^3+2}\,dx=\ln(x^3+2). $$

For the second integral, note that

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(2+e^{-2x})=-2e^{-2x}. $$

Hence

$$ \int \frac{2e^{-2x}}{2+e^{-2x}}\,dx =-\ln(2+e^{-2x}). $$

Therefore,

$$ \int P(x)\,dx=\ln(x^3+2)-\ln(2+e^{-2x}) =\ln\left(\frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\right). $$

So the integrating factor is

$$ \text{I.F.}=e^{\int P(x)\,dx} =\frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}. $$

Step 4: Multiply the equation by the integrating factor

Multiplying throughout by

$$ \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}, $$

we get

$$ \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\frac{dy}{dx} +\frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}P(x)y =(x^3+2). $$

So the left side becomes

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[y\cdot \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\right]. $$

Hence

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left[y\cdot \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}}\right]=x^3+2. $$

Integrating both sides,

$$ y\cdot \frac{x^3+2}{2+e^{-2x}} =\int (x^3+2)\,dx =\frac{x^4}{4}+2x+C. $$

Thus,

$$ y=\frac{2+e^{-2x}}{x^3+2}\left(\frac{x^4}{4}+2x+C\right). $$

Step 5: Use initial condition

Given

$$ y(0)=\frac{3}{2}. $$

Substitute $x=0$:

$$ \frac{3}{2} =\frac{2+e^{0}}{0^3+2}\left(0+0+C\right) =\frac{3}{2}C. $$

So,

$$ C=1. $$

Hence solution is

$$ y=\frac{2+e^{-2x}}{x^3+2}\left(\frac{x^4}{4}+2x+1\right). $$

Step 6: Find $y(1)$

Substitute $x=1$:

$$ y(1)=\frac{2+e^{-2}}{1^3+2}\left(\frac{1}{4}+2+1\right). $$

$$ y(1)=\frac{2+e^{-2}}{3}\left(\frac{1}{4}+3\right) =\frac{2+e^{-2}}{3}\cdot \frac{13}{4} $$

$$ y(1)=\frac{13}{12}(2+e^{-2}). $$

Given that

$$ y(1)=\alpha(2+e^{-2}), $$

so

$$ \alpha=\frac{13}{12}. $$

Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\frac{13}{12}} $$

So, the correct option is D.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{P}(3 \cos \alpha, 2 \sin \alpha), \alpha \neq 0$, be a point on the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1, \mathrm{Q}$ be a point on the circle $x^2+y^2-14 x-14 y+82=0$ and R be a point on the line $x+y=5$ such that the centroid of the triangle PQR is $\left(2+\cos \alpha, 3+\frac{2}{3} \sin \alpha\right)$. Then the sum of the ordinates of all possible points R is:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Centroid :

Let $Q$ be a point on the given circle. First write the circle in standard form:

$$x^2+y^2-14x-14y+82=0 \Rightarrow (x-7)^2+(y-7)^2=16$$

So we can take

$$Q(7+4\cos\theta,\;7+4\sin\theta)$$

Point $R$ lies on the line $x+y=5$. Let its ordinate be $y$, then its abscissa is $5-y$, so

$$R(5-y,\;y)$$

Now use the centroid formula. For triangle $PQR$, centroid is

$$ \left(\frac{x_P+x_Q+x_R}{3},\;\frac{y_P+y_Q+y_R}{3}\right) $$

Given $P(3\cos\alpha,\;2\sin\alpha)$, so the centroid becomes

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{3 \cos \alpha+7+4 \cos \theta+5-y}{3}, \frac{2 \sin \alpha+7+4 \sin \theta+y}{3}\right) \\ & =\left(\cos \alpha+2, \frac{2}{3} \sin \alpha+3\right) \end{aligned} $$

On comparison

Compare the $x$-coordinates:

$$\frac{3 \cos \alpha+7+4 \cos \theta+5-y}{3}=\cos\alpha+2 \Rightarrow 4\cos\theta=y-6$$

So,

$$ \cos \theta=\frac{y-6}{4} $$

Compare the $y$-coordinates:

$$\frac{2 \sin \alpha+7+4 \sin \theta+y}{3}=\frac{2}{3}\sin\alpha+3 \Rightarrow 4\sin\theta=2-y$$

So,

$$ \sin \theta=\frac{2-y}{4} $$

Now use the identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1$:

$$\sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta=1 \Rightarrow(y-6)^2+(y-2)^2=16$$

Simplify:

$$y^2-8 y+12=0$$

This quadratic gives two possible values of $y$ (ordinates of $R$). The sum of roots is $8$, so

$$\Rightarrow \text { Sum of ordinates }=8$$

questions.questionText:
For the function $f:[1, \infty) \rightarrow[1, \infty)$ defined by $f(x)=(x-1)^4+1$, among the two statements:

(I) The set $\mathrm{S}=\left\{x \in[1, \infty): f(x)=f^{-1}(x)\right\}$ contains exactly two elements, and

(II) The set $\mathrm{S}=\left\{x \in[1, \infty): f(x)=f^{-1}(x+1)\right\}$ is an empty set,
options:
only (I) is TRUE

only (II) is TRUE

both (I) and (II) are TRUE

neither (I) nor (II) is TRUE
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For

$$ f:[1,\infty)\to [1,\infty),\qquad f(x)=(x-1)^4+1, $$

we first find the inverse function.

Since for $x\ge 1$, the function $(x-1)^4+1$ is strictly increasing, $f$ is one-one and onto from $[1,\infty)$ to $[1,\infty)$.

Let

$$ y=(x-1)^4+1. $$

Then

$$ y-1=(x-1)^4. $$

Since $x\ge 1$, we take the positive fourth root:

$$ x-1=(y-1)^{1/4}. $$

Hence

$$ f^{-1}(y)=1+(y-1)^{1/4}. $$

So

$$ f^{-1}(x)=1+(x-1)^{1/4}. $$

Now check the two statements one by one.

Statement (I)

We need to solve

$$ f(x)=f^{-1}(x). $$

That is,

$$ (x-1)^4+1=1+(x-1)^{1/4}. $$

So

$$ (x-1)^4=(x-1)^{1/4}. $$

Put

$$ t=x-1 \quad \text{where } t\ge 0. $$

Then the equation becomes

$$ t^4=t^{1/4}. $$

Now solve:

$$ t^4-t^{1/4}=0. $$

This gives

$$ t^{1/4}\left(t^{15/4}-1\right)=0. $$

Hence

$$ t=0 \quad \text{or} \quad t^{15/4}=1. $$

Since $t\ge 0$, from $t^{15/4}=1$ we get

$$ t=1. $$

Therefore

$$ x-1=0 \Rightarrow x=1, $$

or

$$ x-1=1 \Rightarrow x=2. $$

So the set

$$ S=\{x\in[1,\infty): f(x)=f^{-1}(x)\} $$

contains exactly two elements:

$$ S=\{1,2\}. $$

Thus, Statement (I) is TRUE.

Statement (II)

We need to solve

$$ f(x)=f^{-1}(x+1). $$

Now

$$ f(x)=(x-1)^4+1, $$

and

$$ f^{-1}(x+1)=1+\big((x+1)-1\big)^{1/4}=1+x^{1/4}. $$

So the equation becomes

$$ (x-1)^4+1=1+x^{1/4}. $$

Hence

$$ (x-1)^4=x^{1/4}. $$

Again put

$$ t=x^{1/4}, \quad t\ge 1 \text{ since } x\ge 1. $$

Then

$$ x=t^4. $$

So the equation becomes

$$ (t^4-1)^4=t. $$

Instead of solving this directly, test simple values.

For $x=1$:

$$ f(1)=(1-1)^4+1=1, $$

and

$$ f^{-1}(1+1)=f^{-1}(2)=1+(2-1)^{1/4}=2. $$

So $x=1$ is not a solution.

For $x=2$:

$$ f(2)=(2-1)^4+1=2, $$

and

$$ f^{-1}(2+1)=f^{-1}(3)=1+(3-1)^{1/4}=1+2^{1/4}\neq 2. $$

So $x=2$ is not a solution.

Now define

$$ g(x)=(x-1)^4-x^{1/4}, \qquad x\ge 1. $$

We need to check whether $g(x)=0$ has any solution.

Observe:

$$ g(1)=0-1=-1<0, $$

and

$$ g(2)=1-2^{1/4}<0, $$

while

$$ g(3)=16-3^{1/4}>0. $$

Since $g$ is continuous, there is at least one root in $(2,3)$.

So the set

$$ S=\{x\in[1,\infty): f(x)=f^{-1}(x+1)\} $$

is not empty.

Thus, Statement (II) is FALSE.

Therefore, the correct option is

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A: only (I) is TRUE}} $$

questions.questionText: Let for some $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}, f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a function satisfying $f(x+y)=f(x)+2 y^2+y+\alpha x y$ for all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$. If $f(0)=-1$ and $f(1)=2$, then the value of $\sum\limits_{n=1}^5(\alpha+f(n))$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given

$$ f(x+y)=f(x)+2y^2+y+\alpha xy \quad \text{for all } x,y\in \mathbb{R} $$

with

$$ f(0)=-1,\qquad f(1)=2. $$

We have to find

$$ \sum_{n=1}^5 (\alpha+f(n)). $$

Step 1: Use the given relation to find $\alpha$

Put $x=0$ in

$$ f(x+y)=f(x)+2y^2+y+\alpha xy. $$

Then

$$ f(y)=f(0)+2y^2+y+\alpha\cdot 0\cdot y $$

so

$$ f(y)=-1+2y^2+y. $$

Thus, for every real $y$,

$$ f(y)=2y^2+y-1. $$

Now use $f(1)=2$:

$$ f(1)=2(1)^2+1-1=2. $$

This is already satisfied, so now we must use the original equation to determine $\alpha$.

Substitute

$$ f(x+y)=2(x+y)^2+(x+y)-1 $$

and

$$ f(x)=2x^2+x-1 $$

into the given equation:

$$ 2(x+y)^2+(x+y)-1 = 2x^2+x-1+2y^2+y+\alpha xy. $$

Expand the left side:

$$ 2x^2+4xy+2y^2+x+y-1. $$

Right side is:

$$ 2x^2+x-1+2y^2+y+\alpha xy. $$

Comparing both sides,

$$ 4xy=\alpha xy. $$

Hence,

$$ \alpha=4. $$

Step 2: Find $f(n)$ for $n=1$ to $5$

Since

$$ f(n)=2n^2+n-1, $$

we get:

$$ f(1)=2(1)^2+1-1=2 $$

$$ f(2)=2(2)^2+2-1=8+2-1=9 $$

$$ f(3)=2(3)^2+3-1=18+3-1=20 $$

$$ f(4)=2(4)^2+4-1=32+4-1=35 $$

$$ f(5)=2(5)^2+5-1=50+5-1=54 $$

Step 3: Compute the required sum

We need

$$ \sum_{n=1}^5 (\alpha+f(n)). $$

Since $\alpha=4$,

$$ \alpha+f(1)=4+2=6 $$

$$ \alpha+f(2)=4+9=13 $$

$$ \alpha+f(3)=4+20=24 $$

$$ \alpha+f(4)=4+35=39 $$

$$ \alpha+f(5)=4+54=58 $$

Therefore,

$$ 6+13+24+39+58=140. $$

So the required value is

$$ \boxed{140}. $$

Hence, the correct option is B.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{H}: \frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ be a hyperbola such that the distance between its foci is 6 and the distance between its directrices is $\frac{8}{3}$. If the line $x=\alpha$ intersects the hyperbola H at the points A and B such that the area of the triangle AOB is $4 \sqrt{15}$, where O is the origin, then $\alpha^2$ equals
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The given equation of the hyperbola is:

$$ \frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1 $$

Let the uniform eccentricity of the hyperbola be $e$.

According to the problem, the distance between the foci is $6$. For a standard hyperbola, the coordinates of the foci are $(\pm ae, 0)$, so the distance between them is given by $2ae$.

$$ 2ae = 6 $$

$$ ae = 3 \quad \dots (i) $$

The distance between the directrices is given as $\frac{8}{3}$. The equations of the directrices are $x = \pm \frac{a}{e}$, so the distance between them is $2\frac{a}{e}$.

$$ 2\frac{a}{e} = \frac{8}{3} $$

$$ \frac{a}{e} = \frac{4}{3} \quad \dots (ii) $$

To find the value of $a^2$, we can multiply equations (i) and (ii):

$$ (ae) \cdot \left(\frac{a}{e}\right) = 3 \times \frac{4}{3} $$

$$ a^2 = 4 $$

We know the fundamental relationship between $a, b,$ and $e$ for a hyperbola is:

$$ b^2 = a^2(e^2 - 1) $$

$$ b^2 = a^2e^2 - a^2 $$

Using the value $ae = 3$, we have $a^2e^2 = 9$. Substituting $a^2e^2$ and $a^2$ into the equation gives:

$$ b^2 = 9 - 4 = 5 $$

Now, substituting $a^2$ and $b^2$ back into the standard equation, the hyperbola is:

$$ \frac{x^2}{4} - \frac{y^2}{5} = 1 $$

The line $x = \alpha$ intersects the hyperbola at points A and B. Substitute $x = \alpha$ into the equation of the hyperbola to find the y-coordinates of points A and B:

$$ \frac{\alpha^2}{4} - \frac{y^2}{5} = 1 $$

$$ \frac{y^2}{5} = \frac{\alpha^2}{4} - 1 $$

$$ \frac{y^2}{5} = \frac{\alpha^2 - 4}{4} $$

$$ y^2 = \frac{5(\alpha^2 - 4)}{4} $$

$$ y = \pm \frac{\sqrt{5(\alpha^2 - 4)}}{2} $$

So, the coordinates of points A and B are $\left(\alpha, \frac{\sqrt{5(\alpha^2 - 4)}}{2}\right)$ and $\left(\alpha, -\frac{\sqrt{5(\alpha^2 - 4)}}{2}\right)$.

These points lie vertically above and below each other, forming the base of $\triangle AOB$ along the line $x = \alpha$.

Length of the base $AB$ = Distance between A and B = $2 \times \frac{\sqrt{5(\alpha^2 - 4)}}{2} = \sqrt{5(\alpha^2 - 4)}$.

The perpendicular height of $\triangle AOB$ from the origin $O(0,0)$ to the line $x = \alpha$ is simply $|\alpha|$.

We are given that the area of $\triangle AOB$ is $4\sqrt{15}$. Using the area of a triangle formula:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} $$

$$ 4\sqrt{15} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{5(\alpha^2 - 4)} \cdot |\alpha| $$

Squaring both sides to remove the square root and the absolute value:

$$ (4\sqrt{15})^2 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot (\alpha^2) \cdot 5(\alpha^2 - 4) $$

$$ 240 = \frac{5}{4} \alpha^2 (\alpha^2 - 4) $$

$$ 240 \times \frac{4}{5} = \alpha^2(\alpha^2 - 4) $$

$$ 192 = \alpha^4 - 4\alpha^2 $$

$$ \alpha^4 - 4\alpha^2 - 192 = 0 $$

Let $\alpha^2 = t$. This transforms our equation into a quadratic equation:

$$ t^2 - 4t - 192 = 0 $$

Splitting the middle term:

$$ t^2 - 16t + 12t - 192 = 0 $$

$$ t(t - 16) + 12(t - 16) = 0 $$

$$ (t - 16)(t + 12) = 0 $$

So, $t = 16$ or $t = -12$.

Since $t = \alpha^2$ and the square of a real number cannot be negative, we disregard $-12$.

Thus, we have:

$$ \alpha^2 = 16 $$

Option B is the correct answer.

questions.questionText:
Let $\alpha=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{16}+\ldots \infty$ and

$\beta=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{27}+\ldots \infty$. Then the value of

$(0.2)^{\log _{\sqrt{5}}(\alpha)}+(0.04)^{\log _5(\beta)}$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We first find the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

Given

$$ \alpha=\frac14+\frac18+\frac1{16}+\cdots $$

This is an infinite geometric progression with

$$ a=\frac14,\quad r=\frac12 $$

So,

$$ \alpha=\frac{a}{1-r}=\frac{\frac14}{1-\frac12}=\frac{\frac14}{\frac12}=\frac12 $$

Now,

$$ \beta=\frac13+\frac19+\frac1{27}+\cdots $$

This is also an infinite geometric progression with

$$ a=\frac13,\quad r=\frac13 $$

Thus,

$$ \beta=\frac{a}{1-r}=\frac{\frac13}{1-\frac13}=\frac{\frac13}{\frac23}=\frac12 $$

So both are equal to $\frac12$.

Now the expression becomes

$$ (0.2)^{\log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)}+(0.04)^{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)} $$

Convert decimals into fractions:

$$ 0.2=\frac15,\qquad 0.04=\frac1{25}=5^{-2} $$

So,

$$ \left(\frac15\right)^{\log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)}+\left(\frac1{25}\right)^{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)} $$

That is,

$$ 5^{-\log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)}+5^{-2\log_5\left(\frac12\right)} $$

Now use the change of base idea:

$$ \log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)=\frac{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)}{\log_5(\sqrt5)} $$

Since

$$ \log_5(\sqrt5)=\log_5\left(5^{1/2}\right)=\frac12 $$

therefore

$$ \log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)=\frac{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)}{1/2}=2\log_5\left(\frac12\right) $$

Hence first term:

$$ 5^{-\log_{\sqrt5}(1/2)}=5^{-2\log_5(1/2)} $$

So both terms are same:

$$ 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}+5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}=2\cdot 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)} $$

Now use the identity

$$ a^{\log_a x}=x $$

Then

$$ 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}=5^{\log_5(1/2)^{-2}} \text{ is not correct} $$

So let us simplify properly:

$$ -2\log_5\left(\frac12\right)=\log_5\left(\frac12\right)^{-2}? $$

Instead, use

$$ k\log_a b=\log_a(b^k) $$

Thus,

$$ -2\log_5\left(\frac12\right)=\log_5\left(\left(\frac12\right)^{-2}\right)=\log_5(4) $$

Therefore,

$$ 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}=5^{\log_5 4}=4 $$

So the total value is

$$ 2\times 4=8 $$

Hence, the required value is

$$ \boxed{8} $$

So the correct option is Option C.

questions.questionText: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & 7 \\ 4 & -2 & 8 \\ 3 & 8 & -7\end{array}\right]$ and $\operatorname{det}(A-\alpha I)=0$, where $\alpha$ is a real number. If the largest possible value of $\alpha$ is $p$, then the circle $(x-p)^2+(y-2 p)^2=320$, intersects the co-ordinate axes at
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We need the largest real value of $\alpha$ such that

$$ \det(A-\alpha I)=0 $$

That means $\alpha$ is an eigenvalue of the matrix

$$ A=\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&7\\ 4&-2&8\\ 3&8&-7 \end{bmatrix}. $$

So first we find the eigenvalues of $A$.

Step 1: Form $A-\alpha I$

$$ A-\alpha I= \begin{bmatrix} 1-\alpha & 2 & 7\\ 4 & -2-\alpha & 8\\ 3 & 8 & -7-\alpha \end{bmatrix} $$

Now,

$$ \det(A-\alpha I)= \begin{vmatrix} 1-\alpha & 2 & 7\\ 4 & -2-\alpha & 8\\ 3 & 8 & -7-\alpha \end{vmatrix} =0 $$

Step 2: Expand the determinant

Expanding along the first row:

$$ \det(A-\alpha I) =(1-\alpha)\begin{vmatrix}-2-\alpha & 8\\ 8 & -7-\alpha\end{vmatrix} -2\begin{vmatrix}4 & 8\\ 3 & -7-\alpha\end{vmatrix} +7\begin{vmatrix}4 & -2-\alpha\\ 3 & 8\end{vmatrix} $$

Now calculate each minor:

$$ \begin{vmatrix}-2-\alpha & 8\\ 8 & -7-\alpha\end{vmatrix} =(-2-\alpha)(-7-\alpha)-64 $$

$$ =(\alpha+2)(\alpha+7)-64 =\alpha^2+9\alpha+14-64 =\alpha^2+9\alpha-50 $$

Next,

$$ \begin{vmatrix}4 & 8\\ 3 & -7-\alpha\end{vmatrix} =4(-7-\alpha)-24 =-28-4\alpha-24 =-52-4\alpha $$

And,

$$ \begin{vmatrix}4 & -2-\alpha\\ 3 & 8\end{vmatrix} =32-3(-2-\alpha) =32+6+3\alpha =38+3\alpha $$

So,

$$ \det(A-\alpha I) =(1-\alpha)(\alpha^2+9\alpha-50)-2(-52-4\alpha)+7(38+3\alpha) $$

Now simplify:

$$ (1-\alpha)(\alpha^2+9\alpha-50) =\alpha^2+9\alpha-50-\alpha^3-9\alpha^2+50\alpha $$

$$ =-\alpha^3-8\alpha^2+59\alpha-50 $$

Also,

$$ -2(-52-4\alpha)=104+8\alpha $$

and

$$ 7(38+3\alpha)=266+21\alpha $$

Therefore,

$$ \det(A-\alpha I) =-\alpha^3-8\alpha^2+59\alpha-50+104+8\alpha+266+21\alpha $$

$$ =-\alpha^3-8\alpha^2+88\alpha+320 $$

So the equation becomes

$$ -\alpha^3-8\alpha^2+88\alpha+320=0 $$

Multiplying by $-1$:

$$ \alpha^3+8\alpha^2-88\alpha-320=0 $$

Step 3: Factorise

Try integer roots. On putting $\alpha=8$:

$$ 8^3+8(8^2)-88(8)-320=512+512-704-320=0 $$

So $(\alpha-8)$ is a factor.

Divide:

$$ \alpha^3+8\alpha^2-88\alpha-320=(\alpha-8)(\alpha^2+16\alpha+40) $$

Now solve

$$ \alpha^2+16\alpha+40=0 $$

$$ \alpha=\frac{-16\pm\sqrt{256-160}}{2} =\frac{-16\pm\sqrt{96}}{2} =\frac{-16\pm 4\sqrt6}{2} =-8\pm 2\sqrt6 $$

Hence the eigenvalues are

$$ 8,\quad -8+2\sqrt6,\quad -8-2\sqrt6 $$

So the largest possible value is

$$ p=8 $$

Step 4: Put $p=8$ in the circle

Given circle:

$$ (x-p)^2+(y-2p)^2=320 $$

Substitute $p=8$:

$$ (x-8)^2+(y-16)^2=320 $$

Its centre is $(8,16)$ and radius is

$$ r=\sqrt{320}=8\sqrt5 $$

Step 5: Find intersections with the coordinate axes

On the $x$-axis

For points on the $x$-axis, $y=0$.

$$ (x-8)^2+(0-16)^2=320 $$

$$ (x-8)^2+256=320 $$

$$ (x-8)^2=64 $$

$$ x-8=\pm 8 $$

So,

$$ x=16 \quad \text{or} \quad x=0 $$

Thus the circle meets the $x$-axis at

$$ (16,0),\ (0,0) $$

On the $y$-axis

For points on the $y$-axis, $x=0$.

$$ (0-8)^2+(y-16)^2=320 $$

$$ 64+(y-16)^2=320 $$

$$ (y-16)^2=256 $$

$$ y-16=\pm 16 $$

So,

$$ y=32 \quad \text{or} \quad y=0 $$

Thus the circle meets the $y$-axis at

$$ (0,32),\ (0,0) $$

Step 6: Count distinct intersection points

The distinct points are

$$ (16,0),\ (0,0),\ (0,32) $$

So the circle intersects the coordinate axes at

$$ \boxed{3\text{ points}} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option C}} $$

questions.questionText:
If the system of equations :

$$ \begin{aligned} & x+y+z=5 \\ & x+2 y+3 z=9 \\ & x+3 y+\lambda z=\mu \end{aligned} $$

has infinitely many solutions, then the value of $\lambda+\mu$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & D=\lambda-5 \\ & D_1=\lambda+\mu-18 \\ & D_2=4 \lambda-2 \mu+6 \\ & D_3=\mu-13 \\ & \text { Here } x=\frac{D_1}{D} ; y=\frac{D_2}{D} ; z=\frac{D_3}{D} \end{aligned} $$

For the system to have infinitely many solutions, the determinant of the coefficient matrix and all the corresponding determinants must be zero.

$$ \begin{aligned} & D=D_1=D_2=D_3=0 \end{aligned} $$

First, set $D=0$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \lambda-5=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=5 \end{aligned} $$

Next, set $D_3=0$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mu-13=0 \Rightarrow \mu=13 \end{aligned} $$

Now check $D_1=0$ using $\lambda=5$ and $\mu=13$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \lambda+\mu-18=5+13-18=0 \end{aligned} $$

Also check $D_2=0$:

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4\lambda-2\mu+6=4(5)-2(13)+6=20-26+6=0 \end{aligned} $$

So the values are consistent. Therefore,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \lambda+\mu=5+13=18 \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText:
Let

$\mathrm{A}=\{(a, b, c): a, b, c$ are non-negative integers and $a+b+2 c=22\}$.

Then $n(\mathrm{~A})$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We need to count the number of ordered triples $(a,b,c)$ of non-negative integers satisfying

$$ a+b+2c=22 $$

Since $a,b,c \ge 0$, let us fix $c$ first.

For a fixed value of $c$, the equation becomes

$$ a+b=22-2c $$

Now, the number of non-negative integer solutions of

$$ a+b=n $$

is

$$ n+1 $$

So, for fixed $c$, number of solutions is

$$ (22-2c)+1=23-2c $$

Now find all possible values of $c$.

Since $2c \le 22$,

$$ c=0,1,2,\dots,11 $$

Hence total number of solutions is

$$ \sum_{c=0}^{11} (23-2c) $$

This is an arithmetic series:

  • first term $=23$

  • last term $=23-2(11)=1$

  • number of terms $=12$

So,

$$ n(A)=\frac{12}{2}(23+1) $$

$$ n(A)=6 \cdot 24=144 $$

Therefore,

$$ n(A)=144 $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{144} $$

Hence, $\boxed{\text{Option C}}$.

questions.questionText: If the quadratic equation $(\lambda+2) x^2-3 \lambda x+4 \lambda=0, \lambda \neq-2$, has two positive roots, then the number of possible integral values of $\lambda$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For the quadratic equation

$$ (\lambda+2)x^2-3\lambda x+4\lambda=0,\qquad \lambda\neq -2 $$

to have two positive roots, let us use the NCERT conditions for a quadratic equation.

If the roots are $\alpha$ and $\beta$, then for both roots to be positive, we need:

  1. Discriminant $\ge 0$ so that roots are real,

  2. Sum of roots $>0$,

  3. Product of roots $>0$.

1. Identify coefficients

Comparing with $ax^2+bx+c=0$, we get

$$ a=\lambda+2,\qquad b=-3\lambda,\qquad c=4\lambda $$

2. Condition for real roots

Discriminant:

$$ D=b^2-4ac $$

So,

$$ D=(-3\lambda)^2-4(\lambda+2)(4\lambda) $$

$$ D=9\lambda^2-16\lambda(\lambda+2) $$

$$ D=9\lambda^2-16\lambda^2-32\lambda $$

$$ D=-7\lambda^2-32\lambda $$

For real roots,

$$ -7\lambda^2-32\lambda \ge 0 $$

$$ -\lambda(7\lambda+32)\ge 0 $$

Multiplying by $-1$ reverses the inequality:

$$ \lambda(7\lambda+32)\le 0 $$

This gives

$$ -\frac{32}{7}\le \lambda \le 0 $$

So possible integral values from this condition are

$$ \lambda=-4,-3,-2,-1,0 $$

But given $\lambda\ne -2$, so remaining are

$$ \lambda=-4,-3,-1,0 $$

3. Condition for sum of roots positive

Sum of roots is

$$ \alpha+\beta = -\frac{b}{a}=\frac{3\lambda}{\lambda+2} $$

For two positive roots,

$$ \frac{3\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

Since $3>0$, this means

$$ \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

This is positive when numerator and denominator have same sign:

  • $\lambda>0$ and $\lambda+2>0$, or

  • $\lambda<0$ and $\lambda+2<0$

So,

$$ \lambda>0 \quad \text{or} \quad \lambda<-2 $$

From our shortlisted values $-4,-3,-1,0$, this keeps only

$$ \lambda=-4,-3 $$

4. Condition for product of roots positive

Product of roots is

$$ \alpha\beta=\frac{c}{a}=\frac{4\lambda}{\lambda+2} $$

For positive roots,

$$ \frac{4\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

Again,

$$ \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+2}>0 $$

This gives the same condition:

$$ \lambda>0 \quad \text{or} \quad \lambda<-2 $$

Among the shortlisted values, again we get

$$ \lambda=-4,-3 $$

5. Check both values

  • For $\lambda=-4$:

    $$ D=-7(16)-32(-4)=-112+128=16>0 $$

    So roots are real and distinct.

  • For $\lambda=-3$:

    $$ D=-7(9)-32(-3)=-63+96=33>0 $$

    So roots are real and distinct.

Hence both values give two positive roots.

Therefore, the number of possible integral values of $\lambda$ is

$$ \boxed{2} $$

So, the correct option is B.

questions.questionText: For 10 observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{10}$, if $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10}\left(x_i+2\right)^2=180$ and $\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10}\left(x_i-1\right)^2=90$, then their standard deviation is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} (x_i + 2)^2 = 180$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i + \sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} 4 = 180$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 180 - 40 \quad \ldots (1)$$

Also $$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} (x_i - 1)^2 = 90$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i + \sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} 1 = 90$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 90 - 10 \quad \ldots (2)$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 140$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 80$$

$$\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 = 100 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 10$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2}{N} - \left(\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{10} x_i}{N}\right)^2$$

$$= \frac{100}{10} - \left(\frac{10}{10}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma^2 = 10 - 1 = 9$$

$$\Rightarrow \sigma = 3$$

questions.questionText: Let $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$ be unit vectors inclined at an acute angle such that $|\hat{u} \times \hat{v}|=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=\lambda \hat{u}+\hat{v}+(\hat{u} \times \hat{v})$, then $\lambda$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$ are unit vectors,

$$ |\hat{u}\times \hat{v}|=|\hat{u}||\hat{v}|\sin\theta=\sin\theta $$

Given

$$ |\hat{u}\times \hat{v}|=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

So,

$$ \sin\theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

The angle is acute, hence

$$ \theta=60^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{u}\cdot \hat{v}=\cos 60^\circ=\frac{1}{2} $$

Now,

$$ \vec A=\lambda \hat{u}+\hat{v}+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v}) $$

We take dot product with $\hat{u}$:

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{u} = \lambda (\hat{u}\cdot \hat{u})+(\hat{v}\cdot \hat{u})+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{u} $$

Now,

$$ \hat{u}\cdot \hat{u}=1,\qquad \hat{v}\cdot \hat{u}=\frac{1}{2},\qquad (\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{u}=0 $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{u}=\lambda+\frac{1}{2} $$

So,

$$ \lambda=\vec A\cdot \hat{u}-\frac{1}{2} \qquad \text{(1)} $$

Now take dot product with $\hat{v}$:

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{v} = \lambda (\hat{u}\cdot \hat{v})+(\hat{v}\cdot \hat{v})+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{v} $$

Using

$$ \hat{u}\cdot \hat{v}=\frac{1}{2},\qquad \hat{v}\cdot \hat{v}=1,\qquad (\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{v}=0 $$

we get

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{v}=\frac{\lambda}{2}+1 $$

Hence,

$$ \frac{\lambda}{2}=\vec A\cdot \hat{v}-1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda=2(\vec A\cdot \hat{v})-2 \qquad \text{(2)} $$

Now let

$$ x=\vec A\cdot \hat{u}, \qquad y=\vec A\cdot \hat{v} $$

Then from above,

$$ x=\lambda+\frac{1}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad y=\frac{\lambda}{2}+1 $$

From the first,

$$ \lambda=x-\frac{1}{2} $$

Also,

$$ x-2y=\left(\lambda+\frac{1}{2}\right)-2\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}+1\right) =\lambda+\frac{1}{2}-\lambda-2=-\frac{3}{2} $$

So we form $\lambda$ as a linear combination of $x$ and $y$.

Check Option A:

$$ \frac{4}{3}x-\frac{2}{3}y = \frac{4}{3}\left(\lambda+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}+1\right) $$

$$ = \frac{4\lambda}{3}+\frac{2}{3}-\frac{\lambda}{3}-\frac{2}{3} = \lambda $$

Hence,

$$ \lambda=\frac{4}{3}(\vec A\cdot \hat{u})-\frac{2}{3}(\vec A\cdot \hat{v}) $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $$

questions.questionText:
Let $f$ be a twice differentiable function such that

$$ f(x)=\int_0^x \tan (t-x) d t-\int_0^x f(t) \tan t d t, x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) $$

Then $f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+12 f^{\prime}\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ is equal to _______.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let

$$ f(x)=\int_0^x \tan (t-x)\,dt-\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt, \qquad x\in \left(-\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2}\right). $$

We have to find

$$ f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+12f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right). $$

Step 1: Simplify the first integral

Consider

$$ \int_0^x \tan(t-x)\,dt. $$

Put

$$ u=t-x \implies du=dt. $$

When $t=0$, $u=-x$, and when $t=x$, $u=0$.

So,

$$ \int_0^x \tan(t-x)\,dt=\int_{-x}^0 \tan u\,du. $$

Now,

$$ \int \tan u\,du = -\ln(\cos u). $$

Hence,

$$ \int_{-x}^0 \tan u\,du = \left[-\ln(\cos u)\right]_{-x}^0 = -\ln(\cos 0)+\ln(\cos(-x)). $$

Since $\cos 0=1$ and $\cos(-x)=\cos x$,

$$ \int_0^x \tan(t-x)\,dt=\ln(\cos x). $$

Therefore the given equation becomes

$$ f(x)=\ln(\cos x)-\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt. $$

So,

$$ f(x)+\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt=\ln(\cos x). $$

Step 2: Differentiate the equation

Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$.

Using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_0^x f(t)\tan t\,dt\right)=f(x)\tan x. $$

Also,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\big(\ln(\cos x)\big)= -\tan x. $$

Thus,

$$ f'(x)+f(x)\tan x=-\tan x. $$

So,

$$ f'(x)=-(f(x)+1)\tan x. $$

Step 3: Find $f(x)$ explicitly

Now observe that $f(x)=-1$ satisfies the differential equation:

$$ f'(x)=0,\qquad -(f(x)+1)\tan x=0. $$

Also from the original equation, put $x=0$:

$$ f(0)=\int_0^0 \tan(t-0)\,dt-\int_0^0 f(t)\tan t\,dt=0. $$

So,

$$ f(0)=0. $$

Let us solve the differential equation properly.

From

$$ f'(x)+(f(x)+1)\tan x=0, $$

put

$$ y(x)=f(x)+1. $$

Then

$$ y'(x)+y(x)\tan x=0. $$

This gives

$$ \frac{y'}{y}=-\tan x. $$

Integrating,

$$ \ln |y|=\int -\tan x\,dx=\ln(\cos x)+C. $$

Hence,

$$ y=C\cos x. $$

Therefore,

$$ f(x)+1=C\cos x. $$

Using $f(0)=0$,

$$ 0+1=C\cos 0=C. $$

So $C=1$, and hence

$$ f(x)+1=\cos x $$

which gives

$$ f(x)=\cos x-1. $$

Step 4: Find derivatives

Since

$$ f(x)=\cos x-1, $$

we get

$$ f'(x)=-\sin x, $$

and

$$ f''(x)=-\cos x. $$

Now compute required values:

1. $f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$$ f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) =-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}. $$

2. $f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$$ f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-\sin\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) =\frac{1}{2}. $$

So,

$$ 12f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=12\cdot \frac{1}{2}=6. $$

3. $f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$$ f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-1 =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-1. $$

Step 5: Add them

$$ f''\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+12f'\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+f\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) $$

$$ = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+6+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-1\right) $$

$$ = 6-1=5. $$

Hence, the required value is

$$ \boxed{5}. $$

questions.questionText: Let $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}e^{x-1} & , x<0 \\ x^2-5 x+6 & , x \geq 0\end{array}\right.$ and $g(x)=f(|x|)+|f(x)|$. If the number of points where $g$ is not continuous and is not differentiable are $\alpha$ and $\beta$ respectively, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
First look at $|f(x)|$.

For $x<0$, we have $f(x)=e^{x-1}$, which is always positive, so $|f(x)|=e^{x-1}$. This part is continuous and differentiable for all $x<0$.

For $x\\ge 0$, $f(x)=x^2-5x+6=(x-2)(x-3)$. This becomes $0$ at $x=2$ and $x=3$, and it changes sign between $2$ and $3$. So when we take absolute value, $|f(x)|$ gets a “sharp corner” at $x=2$ and $x=3$, hence it is not differentiable at $x=2,3$.

At $x=0$, check continuity of $|f(x)|$: left value tends to $|e^{-1}|=e^{-1}$, but right value is $|f(0)|=|6|=6$. Since these are not equal, $|f(x)|$ is discontinuous at $x=0$ (and therefore also not differentiable at $x=0$).

Now consider $g(x)=f(|x|)+|f(x)|$.

Here $f(|x|)$ always uses the $x\\ge 0$ definition because $|x|\\ge 0$. So $f(|x|)$ is continuous for all $x$, but it is not differentiable at $x=0$ because of $|x|$.

So, discontinuity in $g$ can only come from $|f(x)|$, which happens at $x=0$ only. Also, $g$ will be non-differentiable where either term is non-differentiable, i.e., at $x=0,2,3$.

$\begin{aligned} & |\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})| \text { is discontinuous at } \mathrm{x}=0 \\ & |\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})| \text { is non-diff. at } \mathrm{x}=0,2,3 \\ & \mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}(|\mathrm{x}|)+|\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})| \\ & \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}) \text { will be discontinuous at } \mathrm{x}=0 \\ & \mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{x}) \text { will be non diff at } \mathrm{x}=0,2,3 \\ & \alpha=1, \beta=3 \\ & \alpha+\beta=1+3=4\end{aligned}$

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C be the vertices of a variable right angled triangle inscribed in the parabola $y^2=16 x$. Let the vertex $B$ containing the right angle be $(4,8)$ and the locus of the centroid of $\triangle A B C$ be a conic $C_0$. Then three times the length of latus rectum of $\mathrm{C}_0$ is $\_\_\_\_$
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since the right angle is at $B=(4,8)$, and $A,C$ lie on the parabola

$$ y^2=16x, $$

we first parametrize the parabola in the standard NCERT form.

For $y^2=4ax$, we have $4a=16 \Rightarrow a=4$.

So any point on the parabola can be written as

$$ ( at^2,\, 2at )=(4t^2,\, 8t). $$

Thus let

$$ A=(4t_1^2,\, 8t_1), \qquad C=(4t_2^2,\, 8t_2). $$

Also, the point $B=(4,8)$ lies on the parabola and corresponds to parameter $t=1$, since

$$ (4\cdot 1^2,\, 8\cdot 1)=(4,8). $$

So $B$ is the point with parameter $1$.

Now, for the triangle $ABC$ to be right-angled at $B$, the slopes of $BA$ and $BC$ must satisfy

$$ m_{BA}\, m_{BC}=-1. $$

For parabola $y^2=4ax$, the slope of chord joining parameter points $t_1$ and $t_2$ is

$$ \frac{2a(t_2-t_1)}{a(t_2^2-t_1^2)}=\frac{2}{t_1+t_2}. $$

So slope of chord joining parameter $1$ and $t_1$ is

$$ m_{BA}=\frac{2}{1+t_1}, $$

and slope of chord joining parameter $1$ and $t_2$ is

$$ m_{BC}=\frac{2}{1+t_2}. $$

Since these are perpendicular,

$$ \frac{2}{1+t_1}\cdot \frac{2}{1+t_2}=-1. $$

Hence

$$ \frac{4}{(1+t_1)(1+t_2)}=-1 $$

which gives

$$ (1+t_1)(1+t_2)=-4. $$

So,

$$ t_1t_2+t_1+t_2+1=-4 $$

or

$$ t_1t_2+t_1+t_2=-5. $$

Now let the centroid of $\triangle ABC$ be $G(h,k)$.

Using centroid formula,

$$ h=\frac{4t_1^2+4+4t_2^2}{3} =\frac{4}{3}(t_1^2+t_2^2+1), $$

$$ k=\frac{8t_1+8+8t_2}{3} =\frac{8}{3}(t_1+t_2+1). $$

Let

$$ S=t_1+t_2, \qquad P=t_1t_2. $$

Then from the perpendicularity condition,

$$ P+S=-5. $$

Also,

$$ t_1^2+t_2^2=S^2-2P. $$

So

$$ h=\frac{4}{3}(S^2-2P+1). $$

Since $P=-5-S$,

$$ h=\frac{4}{3}\bigl(S^2-2(-5-S)+1\bigr) $$

$$ h=\frac{4}{3}(S^2+2S+11). $$

Also,

$$ k=\frac{8}{3}(S+1). $$

From this,

$$ S+1=\frac{3k}{8} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S=\frac{3k}{8}-1. $$

Substitute into $h$:

$$ h=\frac{4}{3}\left[\left(\frac{3k}{8}-1\right)^2+2\left(\frac{3k}{8}-1\right)+11\right]. $$

Now simplify:

$$ \left(\frac{3k}{8}-1\right)^2=\frac{9k^2}{64}-\frac{3k}{4}+1 $$

and

$$ 2\left(\frac{3k}{8}-1\right)=\frac{3k}{4}-2. $$

So inside the bracket,

$$ \frac{9k^2}{64}-\frac{3k}{4}+1+\frac{3k}{4}-2+11 = \frac{9k^2}{64}+10. $$

Hence

$$ h=\frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{9k^2}{64}+10\right) =\frac{3k^2}{16}+\frac{40}{3}. $$

Therefore,

$$ h-\frac{40}{3}=\frac{3k^2}{16}. $$

Rearranging,

$$ k^2=\frac{16}{3}\left(h-\frac{40}{3}\right). $$

So the locus is a parabola:

$$ k^2=4a\left(h-\frac{40}{3}\right) $$

with

$$ 4a=\frac{16}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad a=\frac{4}{3}. $$

Length of latus rectum of this parabola is

$$ 4a=\frac{16}{3}. $$

Therefore, three times the length of latus rectum is

$$ 3\times \frac{16}{3}=16. $$

So the required answer is

$$ \boxed{16}. $$

questions.questionText: From a month of 31 days, 3 different dates are selected at random. If the probability that these dates are in an increasing A.P. is equal to $\frac{a}{b}$, where $a, b \in$ N and $\operatorname{gcd}(a, b)=1$, then $a+b$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Total number of ways to select any $3$ different dates from $31$ days is

$$ \binom{31}{3} $$

Now we count the number of selections in which the three dates are in an increasing A.P.

Let the three dates be

$$ a-d,\ a,\ a+d $$

where $d \ge 1$ is an integer.

So we need all triples of the form

$$ x,\ x+r,\ x+2r $$

with

$$ x \ge 1,\quad x+2r \le 31 $$

For a fixed common difference $r$, the first term $x$ can be chosen from

$$ 1,2,3,\dots,31-2r $$

So number of A.P.s with common difference $r$ is

$$ 31-2r $$

Now $r$ can take values

$$ 1,2,3,\dots,15 $$

because

$$ x+2r \le 31 \Rightarrow 2r \le 30 \Rightarrow r \le 15 $$

Hence total favourable selections are

$$ \sum_{r=1}^{15} (31-2r) $$

This is

$$ 15\cdot 31 - 2\sum_{r=1}^{15} r $$

$$ = 465 - 2\cdot \frac{15\cdot 16}{2} $$

$$ = 465 - 240 $$

$$ = 225 $$

Therefore,

$$ P=\frac{225}{\binom{31}{3}} $$

Now,

$$ \binom{31}{3}=\frac{31\cdot 30\cdot 29}{6}=4495 $$

So,

$$ P=\frac{225}{4495} $$

Divide numerator and denominator by $5$:

$$ P=\frac{45}{899} $$

Thus,

$$ a=45,\quad b=899 $$

Therefore,

$$ a+b=45+899=944 $$

So the required answer is

$$ \boxed{944} $$

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ be points on the two half-lines $x-\sqrt{3}|y|=\alpha, \alpha>0$ at a distance of $\alpha$ from their point of intersection $P$. The line segment $A B$ meets the angle bisector of the given half-lines at the point $Q$. If $P Q=\frac{9}{2}$ and $R$ is the radius of the circumcircle of $\triangle \mathrm{PAB}$, then $\frac{\alpha^2}{R}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The two half-lines are given by

$$ x-\sqrt{3}|y|=\alpha,\qquad \alpha>0 $$

We will first understand their geometry.

1. Find the two half-lines and their point of intersection

Since $|y|$ is present, we split into two cases:

Case 1: $y \ge 0$

$$ x-\sqrt{3}y=\alpha $$

Case 2: $y \le 0$

$$ x+\sqrt{3}y=\alpha $$

These are two lines meeting at the point $P$.

To find their intersection, put $y=0$ in either equation:

$$ x=\alpha $$

So,

$$ P=(\alpha,0) $$

Thus the two half-lines start from $P$ and go outward along these two lines.

2. Find points $A$ and $B$

Points $A$ and $B$ lie on these two half-lines and are each at distance $\alpha$ from $P$.

Let $A$ be on the upper line $x-\sqrt{3}y=\alpha$ and $B$ on the lower line $x+\sqrt{3}y=\alpha$.

The slope of the upper line is

$$ y=\frac{x-\alpha}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow m=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} $$

So this line makes angle $30^\circ$ with the positive $x$-axis.

Similarly, the lower line makes angle $-30^\circ$ with the positive $x$-axis.

Hence the angle between the two half-lines is

$$ 60^\circ $$

Since $PA=\alpha$ and $PB=\alpha$, the points are obtained by moving distance $\alpha$ from $P=(\alpha,0)$ along directions $\pm 30^\circ$.

Therefore,

$$ A=\left(\alpha+\alpha\cos30^\circ,\ \alpha\sin30^\circ\right) $$

$$ B=\left(\alpha+\alpha\cos30^\circ,\ -\alpha\sin30^\circ\right) $$

Using $\cos30^\circ=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\sin30^\circ=\frac12$,

$$ A=\left(\alpha+\frac{\sqrt{3}\alpha}{2},\ \frac{\alpha}{2}\right) $$

$$ B=\left(\alpha+\frac{\sqrt{3}\alpha}{2},\ -\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) $$

3. Find the angle bisector and point $Q$

The figure is symmetric about the $x$-axis, so the angle bisector of the two half-lines is the positive $x$-axis.

Also, $A$ and $B$ have the same $x$-coordinate, so $AB$ is a vertical segment.

Hence $AB$ meets the $x$-axis at

$$ Q=\left(\alpha+\frac{\sqrt{3}\alpha}{2},\ 0\right) $$

So,

$$ PQ = \left(\alpha+\frac{\sqrt{3}\alpha}{2}\right)-\alpha = \frac{\sqrt{3}\alpha}{2} $$

Given that

$$ PQ=\frac92 $$

therefore,

$$ \frac{\sqrt{3}\alpha}{2}=\frac92 $$

So,

$$ \sqrt{3}\alpha=9 $$

$$ \alpha=\frac{9}{\sqrt{3}}=3\sqrt{3} $$

Hence,

$$ \alpha^2 = (3\sqrt{3})^2=27 $$

4. Find circumradius $R$ of $\triangle PAB$

In $\triangle PAB$,

$$ PA=PB=\alpha $$

and the included angle

$$ \angle APB=60^\circ $$

So the triangle is actually equilateral, because

$$ AB^2=\alpha^2+\alpha^2-2\alpha^2\cos60^\circ =2\alpha^2-\alpha^2 =\alpha^2 $$

Thus,

$$ AB=\alpha $$

Hence $\triangle PAB$ is equilateral with side $\alpha$.

For an equilateral triangle of side $a$, circumradius is

$$ R=\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} $$

So here,

$$ R=\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{3}} $$

Using $\alpha=3\sqrt{3}$,

$$ R=\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}}=3 $$

5. Compute $\dfrac{\alpha^2}{R}$

$$ \frac{\alpha^2}{R}=\frac{27}{3}=9 $$

Therefore, the required value is

$$ \boxed{9} $$
